title: Verifier-as-position runbook model with DB-backed synthetic observables
context: Runbooks today — whether prose docs, checklists in PRs, or imperative Thoughtbox notebooks — carry position as maintained state. An operator (human or agent) advances a cursor through steps, either explicitly (checkbox, status field) or implicitly (in-head tracking). This produces three recurring failure modes. (1) Agent resumption is O(log-length): new session must re-read the entire thought log or checklist to reconstruct where work stopped. (2) Position can drift from reality — a step can be marked done while the external system state is not in fact consistent with 'done'. This is the anti-lying failure that makes agentic workflows untrustworthy at scale. (3) Re-entry is non-idempotent: rerunning a step is either a no-op (if the system remembers) or duplicates side effects (if it does not). This ADR proposes treating notebook cells as verification functions against external state rather than imperative steps. Position is never persisted; it is derived by running verifiers. First red verifier from the top is the current step; last green verifier is the highest completed step. The substrate for verification queries is Supabase, because (a) it already supports database webhooks and triggers, so external events (Stripe webhooks, Vercel deploys, operator-attested actions) can be funneled into Postgres rows without Thoughtbox hosting any new webhook infrastructure, and (b) it is the existing control-plane persistence layer per the v1 initiative. MCP clients provide the domain-expert query surface: Stripe MCP queries Stripe state, Supabase MCP queries DB state, each domain's expert knows how to interrogate its own reality. Thoughtbox remains a pure reasoning/orchestration layer. This is distinct from ADR-014, which addresses the execution kernel (Effect-TS, durable workflows, manifest-based codebase modification). ADR-014 is about how cells execute; ADR-021 is about what cells mean.

claims.statement: A verifier cell is a pure function of external state — given the same reality, it returns the same verdict with no dependence on prior cell executions or stored cursor
claims.required_evidence: Two independent notebook sessions, opened minutes apart with no shared memory, produce identical GREEN/NO-GO verdicts for the same runbook step when external state has not changed. Demonstrated by resuming session ecbf5d4d's runbook in a fresh Thoughtbox session and observing identical Step 2 verdict output.

claims.statement: Position in a runbook is derivable from verifier outcomes alone — no persisted cursor, no status column, no checkbox state
claims.required_evidence: Given a notebook with N verifier cells, the canonical position-query function returns current_step = index_of_first_red_verifier and completed_steps = index_of_last_green_verifier. Implemented as a helper cell in the reference runbook. No out-of-band state required.

claims.statement: The system cannot claim a step is done without external reality agreeing — the 'done' predicate is the verifier's green return, not an agent assertion
claims.required_evidence: An adversarial test: attempt to mark a step 'done' by any mechanism other than a passing verifier. No such mechanism exists in the model; the only way to produce a green signal is for the verifier to successfully query reality and pass its assertion.

claims.statement: Synthetic observables for non-observable operator actions are implemented as append-only Supabase rows populated by database webhooks or explicit writes; the verifier queries these rows via Supabase REST or Postgres
claims.required_evidence: Reference implementation shows one runbook step whose completion is not directly observable from external system APIs (e.g. 'operator sent beta email') uses a row in a runbook_events(ts, kind, data) table as its observable. The verifier cell queries this table via Supabase REST and asserts the row exists. Operator can write the row from any authenticated client.

claims.statement: Thoughtbox requires no new infrastructure to support verifier cells — in particular, no webhook hosting, no event bus, no cursor storage. Verifiers are ordinary code cells that call MCP tools or HTTP endpoints.
claims.required_evidence: The reference runbook (.specs/launch/stripe-gated-signup-runbook.src.md) executes in the existing notebook runtime with no subsystem additions. Verifier cells use fetch() and/or MCP tool invocations. Nothing in Thoughtbox core is modified.

claims.statement: Verifier cells are idempotent — running them N times produces no side effects in external systems and returns the same verdict for each run
claims.required_evidence: All verifier cells in the reference runbook use only read-path API calls (GET on Stripe, SELECT on Supabase, GET on deployed endpoints). No POST, PUT, DELETE, or destructive operations. Verified by static inspection of the notebook's code cells.

hypotheses.prediction: Agent resumption cost drops from O(session-length) to O(1) when position is verifier-derived. A new session can orient itself with a single query (run all verifiers, report first red) instead of reading the prior thought log.
hypotheses.falsification: Resume a runbook in a fresh Thoughtbox session without giving the agent the session ID. If the agent cannot orient itself correctly by running verifiers alone (i.e. requires access to prior thoughts or external notes), the hypothesis fails.

hypotheses.prediction: Drift detection is automatic and continuous. If a previously-green step becomes red (e.g. an API key was rotated, a deploy was rolled back), the runbook notices on the next run without any explicit monitoring code.
hypotheses.falsification: Take a runbook at end-of-step-5 all green. Manually cause a green step's condition to become false (rotate a key, disable a service). Re-run all verifiers. If the now-invalid step does not return red, the hypothesis fails.

hypotheses.prediction: Non-observable operator actions can always be promoted to observables cheaply via a runbook_events table and database webhooks — the cost of adding an observable is one SQL row, not one schema migration per event type.
hypotheses.falsification: Attempt to add a new non-observable step to an existing runbook using only a new row in an existing runbook_events table (no schema migration). If this cannot express the observable (e.g. because the data shape requires typed columns), the hypothesis fails and a per-event-type schema is needed.

hypotheses.prediction: The verifier model composes cleanly with existing HDD: an ADR hypothesis is a predicate; a verifier cell is the same predicate expressed as executable code. Adopting the verifier model should reduce, not duplicate, the work of writing HDD ADRs.
hypotheses.falsification: Author an HDD ADR whose hypothesis validation steps are implemented as runbook verifier cells. If the verifier cells cannot replace the ADR's validation section verbatim (e.g. because the hypothesis requires evidence the verifier model cannot express), the hypothesis fails.

decisions.choice: Derived from verifier outcomes; never persisted
decisions.rationale: Eliminates cursor-vs-reality drift. Makes resumption O(1). Makes re-entry idempotent by construction.

decisions.choice: Supabase Postgres tables populated by database webhooks or explicit operator writes
decisions.rationale: Supabase already supports webhooks and triggers per the v1 deployment initiative. No new infrastructure. One substrate for all state queries.

decisions.choice: MCP tool calls preferred; HTTP fetch acceptable; no shell/filesystem access required
decisions.rationale: MCP provides domain-expert query surfaces per external system (Stripe MCP, Supabase MCP). Thoughtbox remains a pure orchestration layer. Notebook cells stay small and portable.

decisions.dimension: Relationship to ADR-014 (Agentic Runbooks / Effect-TS kernel)
decisions.rationale: ADR-014 defines how cells execute (durable runtime, pause/resume, manifest generation). ADR-021 defines what cells mean (verifier vs imperative step). A runbook adopting ADR-021 can run on the ADR-014 execution kernel unchanged.

decisions.choice: Explicitly out of scope for this ADR
decisions.rationale: User scope discipline: use existing Supabase webhook infrastructure rather than growing parallel infrastructure inside Thoughtbox. Revisit only if Supabase-based observables prove insufficient in practice.

decisions.choice: Promote to observables via explicit writes to a runbook_events table
decisions.rationale: Forces 'what would prove this is done?' to be a first-class question at runbook design time. If a step cannot be expressed as a verifier, it may be ill-defined and should be decomposed.

consequences: Runbook notebooks become executable specifications: the cells are the acceptance tests for operational work
Agent handoff across sessions costs one orientation query instead of a full log re-read
Operational drift (rotated keys, rolled-back deploys, disabled services) is detected automatically on next runbook execution
Every runbook step has a falsifiable completion criterion at design time — steps without a verifiable completion predicate are caught early
HDD and runbook verifiers converge: the same predicate can serve both ADR hypothesis validation and operational step completion
Thoughtbox does not grow webhook infrastructure; Supabase absorbs that responsibility via existing database webhooks and triggers
Existing Thoughtbox notebook subsystem requires no modification; ADR-021 is purely a semantic/usage convention with demonstration runbook as reference
